---
title: "The R engine — knitr, ggplot2 and the tidyverse"
description: "Survival curves and forest plots, drawn from data by R at render time — no proprietary stats package required."
image: thumbnail.svg
image-alt: "The R engine — knitr, ggplot2 and the tidyverse — Blood Doctor · Quarto"
date: "2026-07-20"
categories: [Engines, Reproducibility]
engine: markdown
---

Quarto's R support is the mature **knitr** engine that powered R Markdown for a
decade. Anything you can do in R — `ggplot2`, `survival`, `gtsummary`,
`meta` — you can do inside a document, with the output embedded automatically.

## A Kaplan–Meier curve, from code

````markdown
```{r}
#| label: fig-km
#| fig-cap: "Overall survival by treatment arm."
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(df, aes(Time, Survival, colour = Arm)) +
  geom_step(linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("#7f0000", "#2e7d32")) +
  theme_minimal()
```
````

Change the dataset, re-render, and the curve redraws itself. The same pattern
produces meta-analysis **forest plots**, `gtsummary` **Table 1**s, and regression
outputs — the staples of a haematology paper.

## Freedom from licences

R and its 20,000 packages are open source. For most analyses I run in clinic and
research, I am no longer tied to a costly proprietary statistics licence: the
computation, the figure and the manuscript now live in one free toolchain.

::: {.callout-tip}
## See it running live
The [**R engine page**](/engines/r.qmd) renders a survival curve and a forest
plot from scratch. The [**flagship essay**](/posts/2026-07-22-knowledge-has-no-limits/)
also embeds both, side by side with Python, Julia and Observable.
:::
